preamble: This transformation describes the denasalization and raising of a nasalized open front unrounded vowel (ã) to an oral open-mid front unrounded vowel (ɛ). This sound change is often observed in languages where nasal vowels undergo shifts in both nasality and vowel height, driven by phonetic and perceptual factors.

examples.note: Acoustic realization of the phonemic nasal vowel /ã/ is at the same vowel height as the oral /ɛ/ by speakers of Santiago Mexquititlán Otomi, indicating a phonetic overlap or near-merger in terms of height, even if phonemically distinct.
An older variety of Gascon, specifically medieval Bayonnese Gascon, is documented to have undergone a raising process where the nasalized open front unrounded vowel [ã] shifted to a mid-front vowel.

sources: The vowel system of Santiago Mexquititlán Otomi (Hñäñho) | Journal of the International Phonetic Association - Cambridge University Press & Assessment
Production and Perception Mechanisms of Sound Change - Institut für Phonetik und Sprachverarbeitung
(PDF) Perceptual constraints and phonological change: A study of nasal vowel height
